sentences.text: In 1943, '44, we work for the Steinbruch I work.
That means I blow the mountain so that people have can get gravels so the Goeth can build himself a road to this garage where HE have the cars, the mechanic working for him.
Once I remember we set the fuses, we connect the fuses together, and the master give us a cigarette to light the fuse.
Because easier with a cigarette to light the fuse.
And we got a red flag.
So anybody coming on the front gate from outside to inside camp, we should stop them.
Because this road is right next to the mountain when we drill the holes.
And anybody can drive there can be killed because the stones flying everywhere, big chunks of rocks.
So I remember I was far away, maybe about a half a mile or maybe a little less. 1,000, 1,200, 1,500 feet.
And that was-- and then we set fire, you know.
That means that we ignited the fuses, and everything is-- you think that we should be watching.
And all the people hide behind the mountain, or behind this, or behind barracks, behind this.

And then when I was maybe about 50 feet from the gate, and I saw from far away that a car, a Mercedes Benz with a big old hard top down, with four people in it and a driver coming straight to the gate.
And you know, by the time, the fuse only take a few seconds there.
They open the fuse, they opened the gate, and he's coming through.
So I said, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
The red flag.
[NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
Dynamite, everything.
But the driver couldn't hear me, so he tried to take a gun, and the driver driving took a gun for me.
I said, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
And then second, this went off.
The mountain went off.
So the driver-- just the rocks were going everywhere.
The driver reversed the car and hit the fence and broke that fence there.
And they head in because the rocks.
The explosion passed me.
It went past me so much because they give me two fuses.
One red, and one green.
Green was powerful, I remember.
So now everything was quiet.
There was Himmler himself.
I remember that.
But, you know, at that time I didn't know him.
Now I see it.
With three other guys.
The guy who have the gun give me a bunch of cigarette and tapped me.
If I let him go through, through the mountain, I don't know what would happen.
But some people say, you should let them go through.
See what would happen.
So this kind of occasion, you know, is in my mind.
Should I let them through, or should I not let them through?
They will kill me, they will kill everybody there, they will kill the whole camp probably there.
So but my order was to not to let nobody through.
So Goeth came in another way, two brote.
Two loaves of brote, like somebody give me $2 million.
Just like that.
And you know, I saved his life.
He said, you know, oh, you know, he was high, you know, nice.
So he knows me.
Goeth knows me, and Schindler.
Because I saw Schindler so many times.
Because he must go through, through me.
I was maybe about 10, 15 feet from the road drilling holes.
And most of the time, one drill, one watch.
[INAUDIBLE] I took over then.
So we were working all together.
So--
Nothing.
But I know that he's a good Jew.
I know that he want to save Jews because a lot of people want to go to him.
A lot of people pay a lot of money to go to his factory.
Because when Goeth come to the factory, to his factory, he was a partner.
That's what we know now.
They each worked together.
But I do remember, there was a Jewish woman who worked with him, beautiful woman.
Because what happened there?
A lot of times, he's supposed to do something to repair.
So they send me that from-- I was not too far, you know.
They send me from that, from that Steinbruch.
And we went there to fix this, help this.
I saw a dog.
A big German Shepard and a big dog.
Brown with white patches there.
And there was a meat, a raw meat, a plate sitting there.
And I said, wow, I'd like to have the meat.
I'd like to have the meat.
Because, you know, when you don't eat, you see your-- I was at that time 65 pounds, age maybe 18.
You can see the ribs.
You can eat nothing.
And don't eat, bacteria growing on your body.
And then you swell twice the size what you have.
So you know that when you swell, that's it.
You cannot survive.
So anything which you grab, anything to eat, you're safe for another week or two.
So when I pass by and I saw the dog far away, so I went in, grabbed that from his plate, grabbed a whole bunch of meat, and went right to my mouth.
And that was delicious.
So a lot of times-- and when I was there, she was there, too.
She made good sandwich.
She gave me bread.
You know, and I helped her what's supposed to be done, you know that.
And I went back.
Because we were very close.
I was from hishouse very, very close.
And we got closer.
His house was here.
When we dynamite, we went closer to his-- this way a little bit, and to his house.
But a lot of times we went in, and we done it all over again.
All over again.
I remember the whole commando, the whole commando came in.
The Goeth stood by the door, and the commander came in from that work, and they carried the food.
When they deliver food to us where we work, we supposed to bring that container back.
So when we work, everybody changed to bring the empty container back to the camp.
So the Goeth said, open the container.
What you have in it?
There was a little soup in it, a little water in it, or maybe them smuggle something.
He took the whole commando, 60 people, told them to turn and undress themself.
And shut-- and took the clothes.
A lot them didn't want to undress themselves.
They said, no.
You want to kill me, kill me like this.
But the Ukrainian, the Russian, they were free to go closer because when you-- you know, when-- I know when a person would die, he would probably like to fight, you know.
So they kill other.
There, there, there.
So they call us.
We're supposed to strip them, un-strip them.
Put them inside through that.
Do the same thing.
Take the clothes away, and bring it to the special place there.
And cover everything like was before, like nothing.
But when I walk on it, I was walking like I walking on a mattress.
You know, everything was shaky.
And you know, we'd done it, and I figured, I said, should I do it?
Why I done it?
If I wouldn't do it, I wouldn't be here.
It was worth it.
It was not worth it.
I have a family now.
I have children, grandchildren, sister, and brother-in-laws.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Worth it or not?
I said, yes, it was worth it.
What can I do?
If I don't do it, somebody else would do it.
I didn't done it because I wanted to do it.
They forced me to do it, or they would kill me, too.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So in 1944 in May, I decided to go out.
Because I know the Russian are coming in.
Auschwitz is very close.
So they ask, we need 1,000 people to get out of Germany, to Germany-- [? arbeit. ?] I went first.
Because we are close to Auschwitz.
I want to be far away from Auschwitz as possible.
So they took they took me to Gross Rosen. 1,000 people went in.
Oh, about 70, 80 died there.
They beat with the whip.
And somebody was whipped, broke his ribs, anything-- they killed them there.
So we probably came three days later, they took us by truck.
And we went to Wustegiersdorf.
It's a small town by Czech border.
And we start working for the OT Supply Department.
Bringing a lot of supply 100 miles deep in that Polish territory out of Poland to the train to Trutnov.
That's the Czech boarder.
And then it was shipped to Germany.
That's one thing I can't understand.
I don't know if-- if that would ever happen to me as a father now, because I have children, I don't know if I would do it.
The Hungarian people came to the camp.
They-- they were-- they came to the camp 1944, May, June, July.
End of the war. 700,000.
So the son with the father came to the camp.
The son was separated from his father.
And he was maybe, I don't know, two, three miles away.
So he run away from-- on the way home, he run away.
And he found out that his father is in our camp.
So he went to the door, to the gate, and he said, I am from this camp, and I want to be with my father.
So they locked him.
That was on Tuesday.
Sunday we didn't work.
They made appeal.
And there was a small, tiny-- like you clean the carpets.
You put on that-- like you know, on the ground outside.
You put the carpet on, and you clean the carpet there.
They took that-- they took the boy and tried to hang him, but he reached this-- he was a little taller.
His leg was touching the ground.
So he told the father, SS said, come here.
I want you to hang this.
Hang your son.
He said, I wouldn't do it.
Hang your son.
They beat him, they kicked them, they put a gun on the table, doing nothing.
And so people said, listen, if you don't do it, they kill you, too.
So your son-- do it.
No, no, no.
Somehow tell him, just lift your feet out so you be hanged.
And this is the thing to see.
It's like somebody have a-- the whole body shake, twisting, shake, you know.
And he hanged him.
And we were standing watching that.
About a few days later, he killed himself.
He couldn't take it.
So father with the son perished.
So this was-- I cannot describe it.
I don't know what I would do it if you tell me hang my son now.
The feeling for the son.
From there-- This was at Trautenau?
No.
This is in Wustegiersdorf.
Trautenau was the border, station border, when we supplied the food with a truck to go to Germany.
There was like a railroad station there 40 kilometer past us.
So we took the supply, brought to our building in the camp.
Outside the camp there was a giant building.
Eight, nine, story building, maybe about half a mile long.
So we bring supplies from the Polish territory deep, to Czech territory, and then [? so ?].
We drove the trucks to the Trautenau station, railroad station, and there were the trains went to Germany.
They want to bring the food, not for the Russian.
The Russian was there.
So they want to take out from the Russian sector because they knew that Russia, they're very close.
In 1944, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] October, beginning of October, they tell us we march.
We march to Trautenau.
So we march to Trautenau.
They took us on the train.
We went to [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Czech border, too.
I forgot the camp.
It would come to me later.
So when we arrived to the camp, there were a lot of dead people.
A lot of dead people.
It took us three days.
No water.
A lot of dead people from the train?
A lot of dead people.
On the train.
A lot of dead people.
Terrible.
There was no water.
There was only one buckets.
And can you imagine that?
The door was closed.
It was terrible. 100 people, 90 people to our tank, and there's one bucket.
There's no water, no food, no nothing.
The door was closed.
And then we went to Flossenburg.
There was a Flossenburg.
The camp was Flossenburg.
There was concentration camp.
They stripped  us, they washed us, a lot of people got killed.
They put us through quarantine, you know, special camp.
And then they took us out with the trucks to Germany, in Germany.
And then we worked by the railroad station.
But what happened when we fixed it, we fixed the tracks, US plane came in and bombed the tracks.
We fix it, they bomb it.
We fix it, they bomb it.
And they're like a cats and mouse we're playing.
But what happened, there were a lot of Germans-- a lot of Germans anti-government.
Anti-German government.
Political.
But they have the difference.
They wear a David Star, politicals different, red communist.
And they were terrible.
They were completely terrible.
When the United States bombed the railroads, they killed a lot of them. 90% of the German killed.
So we were so happy that they killed them that they would not kill us because they were murderers.
They kill us, they butcher us.
And the time come in that we should walk because the German are very close.
November.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] December, November.
I mean, the Russians are very close--
And the Germans said we're supposed to go.
And so the alarm was signaled there.
In the morning, we wake up, we're supposed to march.
So we march to Landshut, Regensburg.
We were there-- so the city where they took us was Regensburg.
Big city.
And we walked.
But what happened, I found out, they were killing-- we were walking by daytime, but night time we sleep.
So daytime was no good because the fliers-- US Fliers, English Fliers-- pick up a lot of SS.
You know, the SS was not close to us.
They were far away.
About 20, 30 feet.
So they picked up the SS, and they kill a lot of guard, Russian, SS guard.
Excuse me.
So they decided we will sleep daytime and walk night time.
Walk night time.
So every day they took-- we walked in fours.
Just four in a one lane.
So they took five or ten lanes from the back, and they shot them right in the back.
So I found out.
It's no good.
We want to hide.
So I went with my friend.
We hide in that-- we were by the farmers.
So there was a farm.
And they would fight, and there were the cows, and the chicken where we hide.
But the German found us, and they hit me with a rifle, and it cut my head, split my head.
But I have a blanket.
And then I was running right to the column.
The column was maybe about 100 feet away.
I was running.
Oh, maybe about two hours later, the German, one German, a terrible guy.
Oh, are you the guy who were hiding there?
And I said, no.
You a Jew?
No, I'm not a Jew.
And I pushed him away and I fight with him.
Because he was the same with me.
But he got authority, you know, to bring the Jews to the thing so they can be shot.
So I pushed them, fight them.
He let me go.
I don't know how.
He let me go because I said, no, I'm not.
And I talked then Polish good.
So there was a friend of mine.
Shiker, Barba Shiker.
Friend of mine, next thing.
He went in and said, come on, you're the guy.
So they took.
So they went without fighting, without nothing, they went, and they were killed.
So we decided the same night, we're running away.
There is no cheating.
Tomorrow he will find me because I got a scar here, and we're running.
So we run away the same night.
We run, and I put my blanket on my face.
You can see a little bit in the forest.
Not too much, but you can see it.
I hit the tree a lot of times.
But I run through the forest.
That forest-- the branches chopped everything what I have.
The blanket, you know, my some uniform.
And we run maybe for two, three miles.
There were five of us.
And then it's moisture, and its a little wind.
So we decided we would break the trees.
You know, that for Christmas tree.
You know, the branches are very thick.
It was hard for us to break because they were wet.
We couldn't twist it.
We have a spoon and a knife.
Once I use spoon to eat and one side is a knife.
Now, how can you cut with a spoon?
But somehow we done it.
We twisted it around, and we crack it.
Fill up much higher from the ground, maybe about two feet.
Put the two blankets and three blankets, we cover ourselves.
But the wind was terrible.
So we decided to block the wind.
So it would not blow to us-- with one blanket.
Then we switched the places, switched the places to find the bushes so we can block the wind.
Because the wind is the worst thing.
The wind was blowing.
That was in December.
In December.
End of November, December.
November.
So we were already-- it was warm.
The difference was terrific.
The difference from the wind blowing not to blow.
But we were hungry.
So I sleep to 8 o'clock, 9 o'clock.
Nobody.
Oh, it was free.
It was free.
It was free.
It was so good.
I didn't care for the food.
I didn't care what it is.
But I can sleep.
Nobody forced me.
Nobody tell me what to do, how to do, where to go.
And the stomach would say, I'm hungry.
All right, all right.
You know.
So now we went to the farmer and we wanted to something to eat.
Well, well, yeah.
By that time-- and not just by the time we reached, went out from the forest, the dog bark.
He could spot us a mile away.
Bark, the dog.
Bark, bark.
When you're hungry, you must go.
So we went to this farmer sometimes.
We went to farmer.
He give us, throw bread, little bits that were split.
But I remember once when I was in the forest, and we eat in the forest bread, and we cooked potatoes.
But we didn't have no matches.
And we tried to do what the Indians done.
Tried to make fire.
No such a thing.
Oh, impossible.
Supposed to be a magician to make fire.
So I see a lady, a woman there baking bread in the morning.
And I see she put the whole bread, giant bread on the window to cool off.
And she put something, covered like a blanket, I forgot what was it.
But I saw steam coming out.
I said, let's see what's going on there.
So I went with somebody else, and we see bread.
And the bread was hot.
So we took the bread with the whole thing and we went to the forest.
By the time we went to the forest, she came out.
In German, where's the bread?
I have a bread here.
Who took the bread?
The bread is gone.
And we were laughing.
You know, from thing we were laughing.
What is it, 18, 19 years.
What do we know at that time?
And we ate bread, and then we had potatoes, then we cut chickens there.
But we went to another German.
You dirty Jews.
I will kill you.
I said, we'll be back.
After the war is over, we'll be back.
Don't you forget it.
We'll be back.
Well, well, I shoot you.
I call the SS.
Now we in the forest sleeping.
And we see whistles, whistles, whistles.
The German dogs.
They operate by the whistle.
And you whistle to him.
We can hear the whistle in the forest for miles.
So what happened there?
We were sitting there.
And when we looked at the forest, we made a track that everybody can follow us because it was snow.
So we took the branches, and we went this way.
We make a track this way.
We make tracks so that the Germans wouldn't know where we are.
Tracks going this way, tracks going this way, you know, like a map.
And we saw far away that the German dog coming full speed to us.
So we prepare ourselves.
We got the branches.
Good branches, a piece of wood so we can protect ourselves.
No knives, no guns, no nothing.
So I said to us, remember, we know what the German dogs do to us.
And we stay, and we run.
They will kill us.
He will tear a piece of meat off us, and we dead.
Let's attack him.
Let's attack him when he come closer to us.
We are five.
We hit him with that branch or thing.
We kill them.
All right.
Everybody was behind it.
Tree quiet, and the dog going full speed.
When he come close to us, 20 feet, we attack him from nowhere.
Like I wanted him to get a heart attack because he didn't know what happened.
He always attacked the Jews, and non-Jews, or Christian, or Gypsy, or Russian, anybody there.
And tore flesh and didn't eat.
Just tore to pieces.
So that guy was dead.
German came in, and killed him, and took him to the crematorium, and that's OK.
So we know that we have no doctors, and we cannot run away because this is impossible.
So let's see.
So now when we attack him and I hit him, this dog went down.
[WHELPING] And he scream.
So I figure myself, and I try to come closer, you know, and try to pet him.
He forget about the whistle, he forgot about this, this, his owner.
He was, he was one of us.
Nice, fine, beautiful.
No aggressive, no violent, no nothing.
So friendly, so good.
And the German was whistle, whistle, and that whistle disappeared.
And he was afraid to go after us.
Because he didn't know how many escaped. 5, or 10, or 12.
So they know that.
So we went to our lady, and she said, you want to sleep here, go sleep here.
She was maybe about 75.
She had potatoes.
And she have a cow-- three of them.
She have food for the cows to eat.
Did she realize that you were run away Jews?
Yeah.
But she [INAUDIBLE] she didn't care because she couldn't take care of this.
So she said, all right, you eat.
What we eat, you eat.
And I'm looking at a picture.
His son is SS.
SS is-- they swore their legion to Hitler.
They never surrender.
They obey, obey command without question.
Anything.
If they tell him, kill the mother, probably they would do it.
I don't know.
So now so we supposed to go ahead and milk the cow.
And we got a bucket of it.
My friend, young boy.
After we make the bucket, we put the bucket on the side.
He put his nose in it, and drinked the milk right from the bucket.
He didn't survive.
He died.
A few days later he died.
He got loose stomach.
Nothing to stop that because we never got used to it, to the fat.
We never eat fat.
Only dry.
So we found out this is danger.
There was an older guy from us, a little more older than us.
Understand a little bit.
He said, let's make it potato.
A little bit milk. 90% potatoes and a little milk.
Slowly, slowly, slowly, slowly the stomach should get used to it.
And we took bread and sometimes shared a ham.
Sometimes they were hanging that to dry out from a pig.
A whole leg, you know.
So now we like to eat this because they would give us this.
So slowly, slowly.
But he couldn't survive.
Whatever we done to him.
He wouldn't survive.
He died.
So there were four of us.
And we were there together in January, February, March.
April a Wehrmacht commander, maybe about 50 of them, with trucks, with cars, with machine guns, with guns came in.
Everything.
And they brought so much food to them.
And they brought a big kettle to cook the food.
Potato we have plenty, carrots we have plenty, and they have a lot of things.
A lot of meat.
So they kill a lot of chickens and meat that we supposed to clean everything.
I helped them to peel the potatoes.
So I worked with them.
All of us worked with them, with the Wehrmacht.
And they put everything on one pot.
[NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]
After they cooked so good food.
I mean, so much fats.
I took a full plate, and I ate it, and I felt that my body grow, all my disease disappear.
Everything what I have is coming down.
I'm feeling-- and excuse my expression.
When I went out, I feel that I know that I'm not hungry.
That I'm a human being.
You know like this, because you never have a-- you have always problem with it.
So from that time, we were there.
And the Germans surrender.
They run away.
They took all the machine gun and buried in the forest.
So when we went to the-- when we went to-- we found out that the April [INAUDIBLE].
And we saw truck, tank, trucks, tanks on the road, maybe about two miles, a mile and a half from us.
So we walk.
We walked to the road, and we saw a lot of US soldiers.
So we talked Polish, or Jewish, or German.
So we found some lieutenant who was Jewish.
And he spoke, you know, German, too.
So we talked to him.
We asked him where we can get gasoline, gas.
A 5 gallon gas container, they're always carried on a truck.
But the column was stuck.
Nobody can go no place.
Something-- ahead something happened.
They don't know what to.
There was no place where to park the trucks or something.
So we went back to the farmers who didn't treat us and told us a lot of things.
Emptied the whole farmhouse and burned the whole house to the ground.
Two of them.
Two of them.
All house to the ground.
And teach them.
Teach them what the Nazi done to us.
And beat them up completely.
I mean good.
But we couldn't kill.
It was hard to-- especially a woman.
We couldn't hit a woman.
I couldn't.
I couldn't.
But a man, yes.
Because you see, I'm a dirty Jew.
You refused to give me a piece of bread.
Well, I didn't know.
I didn't know.
We beat him very badly.
And then we come back, and the column was still standing there.
So they took us to Laufen.
Took us with the Colonel to Laufen, and we occupy school.
So they gave us a room.
All four in one room.
And the soldiers-- they kept in the soldiers occupied the school in it.
So one friend of mine, he said, he doesn't want to stay with us.
He want to go to look his brother, mother, sister.
But we stayed there.
And we told the Lieutenant that we know where the machine gun and guns are buried because the German buried the machine gun.
But we didn't know exactly where, but approximately.
But when you dig a hole, you see it.
You can see it.
So now the CIA came to us.
He said, what happened?
Machine gun?
Yeah, let's go with us.
You work for us.
So we worked for CIA.
We went to the forest.
We looked for hours, but we found it.
Because we found it.
There was a snow a little bit.
We found that the ground was dig out.
So they took a lot of soldiers, and people would dig this out.
We took all the guns on the truck.
So now we're working for the CIA.
May.
May, May, May.
May.
May.
April.
And then they give us the car.
We work for them.
We could drive with the car.
Everyplace look for the guns where the German hidden their guns, and this, and this.
And then my wife came into the city.
Because not far from us, there was a camp, Lebanon camp.
There was a camp for the mentally sick people there, for the Germans.
[INAUDIBLE] a lot die because they found something.
They found something that they think that it's food, and they die because they shouldn't eat so much.
That's after the war.
No.
Right.
